title: Dog Maggots in Wound (Myiasis): Emergency Guide and Triage Steps
meta_desc: If you see maggots in your dog's wound, it is a high-priority medical emergency. Learn about Myiasis and why immediate vet care is required.
content_html:
What is Myiasis (Maggot Infestation)?

Finding maggots in your dog's open sore can be a frightening and distressing experience. This condition is medically known as Myiasis. It occurs when flies are attracted to a wound, damp fur, or skin infection and lay their eggs there. Within hours, these eggs hatch into larvae (maggots) that begin to feed on the dog's tissue.

Is This an Emergency?

Yes. This is considered a High Urgency emergency. While the maggots themselves are visible on the surface, they often burrow deep into the tissue where they can cause extensive damage, lead to secondary bacterial infections, and release toxins into your dog's bloodstream. Without immediate veterinary intervention, a dog can go into shock or develop life-threatening sepsis. Please contact an emergency veterinarian immediately.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

When you call an emergency clinic, the triage nurse may request a clear photo of the wound. A photo allows the veterinary team to quickly assess the severity of the infestation, the health of the surrounding tissue, and the potential depth of the wound. This information helps them prioritize your dog’s arrival and prepare the specific cleaning solutions and surgical tools needed to treat the area effectively the moment you arrive.

faqs.question: Can I remove the maggots myself at home?
faqs.answer: It is not recommended. Attempting to remove them can cause the larvae to burrow deeper or break apart, leading to worse infection. A veterinarian needs to sedate the dog to properly debride the wound and ensure every larva is removed.

faqs.question: Will peroxide or alcohol kill the maggots?
faqs.answer: No, you should avoid putting harsh chemicals like peroxide or alcohol into a deep wound, as this can damage the healthy tissue and cause significant pain to your dog. Keep the area covered loosely and head to the vet.

faqs.question: What causes Myiasis in dogs?
faqs.answer: Myiasis is usually caused by flies being attracted to moisture, such as blood from a cut, urine-soaked fur, or a skin infection. It is more common in the summer months or in dogs with mobility issues who cannot groom themselves properly.
